coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots derived from glycine for rapid detection of caffeine in beverages
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Duyen H.H.
El-Ramady, Hassan
Prokisch, József
coredata.dc:description: Caffeine, a widely consumed stimulant, requires precise monitoring in food and beverages to ensure consumer safety. However, conventional detection methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), are time-consuming, costly, and equipment-intensive. To overcome these limitations, we present a novel fluorescent sensing platform based on glycine-derived carbon nanodots (Gly-CNDs) for rapid and cost-effective caffeine detection. Unlike existing sensors, our food-grade Gly-CNDs offer enhanced biocompatibility, stability, and selectivity while eliminating the need for complex synthesis or toxic reagents. The synthesized Gly-CNDs, with an average size of 4.2 ± 1.2 nm, exhibited a significant fluorescence enhancement upon caffeine binding, enabling highly sensitive detection within 39–2060 nM range with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 36.5 nM. Tested with beverage samples, this green method offers a promising alternative for monitoring caffeine in foods and could be applied to detect other contaminants and bioactive compounds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Green synthesis
Glycine-derived carbon dots
Caffeine detection
Fluorescence sensing
Food analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Production and characterization of carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs)
Measurement of caffeine content using the HPLC system
Characterization of carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs)
Stability of carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs)
Building the assay for measuring caffeine using carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs)
Proposed mechanisms for carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs) fluorescence enhancement by caffeine
Optimization of experimental conditions for caffeine detection using carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs)
Detection of caffeine in commercial beverages and compared with HPLC method

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the experimental design for developing methods to rapidly detect caffeine in foods using glycine-based carbon nanodots (Gly-CNDs). The design includes three main stages: A. Production and characterization of Gly-CNDs, B. Development of a method to rapidly detect caffeine in beverages using Gly-CNDs, and C. Validation of the proposed methods.

Characterization of carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs) including A, TEM images with 10 nm scale. B, size distribution histogram with the average size at 4.2 ± 1.2 nm; C, the emission fluorescent spectra at different excitation wavelengths (righ t) and UV spectra with real sample under UV lamp (left). D, Raman spectra. E, Carbon and nitrogen percentage. F, FTIR spectra of Gly-CNDs (blue line) and glycine (red line). G, X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra. H, HPLC- SEC-FD chromatogram of isolated Gly-CNDs before the final purification.

Stability of carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs). A, Gly-CNDs in different NaCl concentrations ranging from 0.1 M to 1 M. B, Stability of Gly-CNDs over time (min). C, Stability of Gly-CNDs in different pH conditions. D, the emission fluorescence spectra of Gly-CNDs at the excitation wavelength 360 nm with pH ranging from 4.0–11.0. All samples were measured in triplicate. Error bars expressed the difference of means. Letter a-i indicates the significant differences at p < 0.05.

Selectivity of carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs) in the presence of other interfering compounds at the concentration of 500 μM. A, the relative fluorescence intensity changes of the interfering compound’s response (I/I0 (%)) before and after interacting with Gly-CNDs. B, the emission fluorescence spectra of other compounds after mixing with Gly-CNDs at the excitation wavelength 360 nm. All samples were measured in triplicate. Error bars expressed the difference between means. The asterisk (*) indicates a significant difference from the control sample (Gly-CNDs) (*, p < 0.05 and ****, p < 0.001).

Emission spectra of carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs) at the excitation wavelength 360 nm, including (1) Gly-CNDs with caffeine (1717 nM) added, (2) only Gly-CNDs without caffeine added, and (3) caffeine solution (1717 nM) without Gly-CNDs.

A, Plot of enhancing fluorescence efficiency of carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs) against the caffeine concentration including 0, 39, 129, 154, 206, 309, 412, 514, 687, 1373, 1717, and 2060 nM. B, Emission spectra of Gly-CNDs with caffeine at various concentrations ranging from 0 to 2060 nM at the excitation wavelength at 360 nm. C, Emission spectra of Gly-CNDs with caffeine at various concentrations ranging from 0 to 309 nM at the excitation wavelength at 360 nm. All samples were measured in triplicate. Error bars expressed the difference of means. F and F0 present the fluorescent intensity with and without caffeine.

Optimum experiment conditions for detecting caffeine using carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs). A, Stability in different pH conditions ranging from pH 3.0–11.00. B, the emission fluorescence spectra at the excitation wavelength 360 nm with pH ranging from 4.0 to 11.0. C, Fluorescence intensity over incubation time at 40 °C. D, Fluorescence intensity at different incubation temperatures after 25 minutes. Caffeine (1717 nM) was used for all experiments. All samples were measured in triplicate. Error bars expressed the difference of means. Letters a- g indicate the significant differences at p < 0.05.

Measurement of caffeine in commercial beverages. A, the emission fluorescent spectra of sample 1 with and without Gly-CNDs were added. B. The caffeine concentration of three beverage samples was measured using Gly-CNDs with fluorescence spectrophotometer and HPLC methods. Three energy drinks were selected for this measurement, including sample 1: HELL energy drink- classic boxing kings editions (HELL Magyarország Ltd., Hungary); sample 2: XIXO energy drink original flavor (XIXO Company Ltd., Hungary); sample 3: energy drink apple flavor (Hansen Natural Company, USA); sample 4: green tea (TenRen Co., Taiwan); sample 5: black tea (TenRen Co., Taiwan); sample 6: olong tea (Cau Dat farm Co., Da Lat, Vietnam); sample 7: Commercial fake coffee (Coffee mixed with burnt corn from Vietnam market). All samples were measured in triplicate. Error bars expressed the difference of means. Letter a- I and indicate the significant differences at p < 0.05. ns is abbreviated as “not significant differences.”.
Comparison of different CNDs for the detection of caffeine.
Recoveries of caffeine in beverages detected by the proposed method (n = 5).

row.entry: Energy drink apple flavor (Hansen Natural Company, USA)

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots derived from glycine for rapid detection of caffeine in beverages

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Caffeine, a widely consumed stimulant, requires precise monitoring in food and beverages to ensure consumer safety. However, conventional detection methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), are time-consuming, costly, and equipment-intensive. To overcome these limitations, we present a novel fluorescent sensing platform based on glycine-derived carbon nanodots (Gly-CNDs) for rapid and cost-effective caffeine detection. Unlike existing sensors, our food-grade Gly-CNDs offer enhanced biocompatibility, stability, and selectivity while eliminating the need for complex synthesis or toxic reagents. The synthesized Gly-CNDs, with an average size of 4.2 ± 1.2 nm, exhibited a significant fluorescence enhancement upon caffeine binding, enabling highly sensitive detection within 39–2060 nM range with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 36.5 nM. Tested with beverage samples, this green method offers a promising alternative for monitoring caffeine in foods and could be applied to detect other contaminants and bioactive compounds.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Caffeine, 1,3,7-Trimethylpurine-2,6-dione, a naturally occurring purine alkaloid, is one of the most widely consumed psychoactive substances globally (Samoggia and Rezzaghi, 2021). Estimates suggest that over 80 % of the world's population consumes caffeine daily, primarily through beverages like coffee, tea, and energy drinks (Samoggia and Rezzaghi, 2021). This popularity stems from caffeine's stimulating effects on the central nervous system, leading to increased alertness, improved cognitive function, enhanced physical performance (Portillo and Arévalo, 2022), and reduced cancer risk (Zhao et al., 2024). Despite these health benefits of caffeine, consuming high amounts of caffeine can lead to acute symptoms like hypokalemia, vomiting, nausea, palpitations, seizures, and dysrhythmia (Willson, 2018). In addition to coffee overdose, coffee adulteration is another problem that needs attention. Coffee adulteration, a widespread issue in the industry, involves mixing low-value materials into roasted and ground coffee to reduce costs (Perez et al., 2023). Common adulterants include spent coffee grounds, cereals, legumes, and coffee husks (Cheah and Fang, 2020). This highlights the need for rapid caffeine detection, as it indicates adulteration and helps prevent caffeine overdose. Hence, developing accurate and efficient methods for quantifying caffeine content is essential.

Traditional methods for detecting caffeine typically rely on chromatographic methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Madison et al., 1976), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS), or thin-layer chromatography (TLC) (Torres Y Torres et al., 2015). While HPLC offers high accuracy and sensitivity, it requires expensive equipment, trained personnel, and relatively lengthy analysis times. This can be a significant disadvantage in situations demanding rapid on-site detection or real-time monitoring of caffeine content. Recently, various methods have been developed for detecting caffeine, such as electrochemical sensors (Zhang et al., 2022; Saraban et al., 2024), fluorescent sensors (Xu et al., 2013) and mass spectrometry (Yokokawa et al., 2024). While several sensors, including Cu-MOF/graphene electrochemical sensors (Zhang et al., 2022) and BODIPY-based fluorescent sensors (Xu et al., 2013), have been developed for caffeine detection in beverages, they frequently necessitate complex synthesis, expensive materials, or extensive sample preparation. Consequently, a rapid, cost-effective, and eco-friendly carbon dot-based detection method is greatly needed.

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) have recently garnered attention as promising candidates for sensing applications due to their unique optical properties, including intense fluorescence, high photostability, and biocompatibility (Xu et al., 2004). Due to its strong fluorescence and ability to quench and enhance the fluorescence with a wide range of components, numerous studies were focused on developing CND-based sensors to monitor food components (Lin et al., 2019; Rossini et al., 2019) and detect food contaminants (Vadia et al., 2023; Xavier et al., 2018). Various synthesis methods have been employed to produce CNDs, including hydrothermal treatment (Algarra et al., 2014), plasma, electrochemical oxidation, and thermal decomposition (Wang et al., 2017). In our previous publication (Nguyen et al., 2024a, 2024b ), the safety of using CNDs was still controversial and necessitates further research. Prioritizing the selection of CNDs from safe sources is essential to mitigate potential risks. The majority of CNDs detected in foods have been attributed to the formation of Maillard reactions like bread (Sk et al., 2012), caramelized sugar (Sk et al., 2012), coffee (Costa et al., 2022; Nguyen et al., 2024b), roasted chicken breast (Song et al., 2018), and roasted salmon (Song et al., 2019). Therefore, glycine-sugars, which serve as the primary model of the Maillard reaction in foods, were selected as precursors to synthesize safe CNDs for food applications. Furthermore, among these sources, glycine and sugars present an attractive, sustainable, and cost-effective precursor for CND synthesis (Nguyen et al., 2024c).

In this study, a rapid method to detect and measure caffeine in beverages was developed with focus on the following topics: (1) CNDs were derived from glycine and characterized in size, morphology, and optical properties. (2) The properties of carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs) were assessed under different environmental conditions, including varying NaCl concentrations, pH levels, and over time. (3) The quantitative methods were developed to detect caffeine with Gly-CNDs. (4) Finally, the accuracy and reliability of the proposed method were validated by detecting caffeine in actual beverage samples and comparing the results with traditional HPLC measurements. The findings from this study highlight the potential of using Gly-CNDs as a rapid, efficient, and cost-effective tool for rapid caffeine detection, which could significantly benefit quality control processes in the food and beverage industry. This work not only provides a novel application for Gly-CNDs but also contributes to the broader field of nanotechnology-based sensing methods for food safety and quality assurance.

Coffee beans (Coffea arabica L.) originated from Peru and were purchased from ALDI supermarket in Essen, Germany (roasting level 8/10). Three types of energy drinks were bought to test the caffeine in actual samples from different companies, including HELL ENERGY Magyarország Ltd., Hungary; XIXO Company Ltd., Hungary; Hansen Natural Company, USA. Ultrapure water was used for all experiments. VWR International (Pennsylvania, USA) supplied all chemicals, including 0.01 mol/L hydrochloric acid solution, ≥ 99.5 % ethanol solution, 0.01 mol/L sodium hydroxide solution, and ≥ 99.5 % HPLC-grade acetonitrile. Caffeine standard (analytical grade) was bought from Sigma-Aldrich company in Missouri, USA.

Fig. 1 illustrates the experimental design to develop a method for rapidly detecting caffeine content in beverages using carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs). (1) First, the Gly-CNDs were synthesized and characterized using a transmittance electron microscopy (TEM), fluorescence spectrophotometer, and UV-Vis spectrophotometer. The stability of Gly-CNDs in different conditions was tested, including NaCl concentrations ranging from 0.1 M to 1 M, pH conditions from 3.0 to 11.0, and stability over time. (2) Gly-CNDs were used to develop a method for detecting caffeine content using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The caffeine standard was added into Gly-CNDs from 0 to 7500 µM. (3) the selectivity of the proposed method was assessed along with the stability of Gly-CNDs in the presence of caffeine. (4) Finally, this method was used to measure the caffeine content in energy drinks and then compared with the results of the HPLC method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production and characterization of carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Gly-CNDs were synthesized by mixing glycine and dextrose in a 4:1 ratio. The mixture was then subjected to pyrolysis at 180°C for 2 hours in an oven, ensuring the reaction occurred in the absence of oxygen. After the reaction, 2 L of ultrapure water was added and sonicated for 30 min to dissolve the sample. Then, the isolated Gly-CNDs solution was obtained after passing the microcrystalline cellulose column completely and filtering with a 0.22 μm filter (VWR International Co., Ltd., Pennsylvania, USA). The sample was purified using the HPLC system (ECOM ECB2000, Praha, Czech Republic) with a size-exclusion column 60 × 21.20 mm (Phenomenex BioSep-SEC-S 2000 60 ×21.20 mm, California, USA) connected with a RF-20A XS fluorescent detector (Shimadzu Co., Kyoto, Japan). Gly-CNDs were lyophilized and stored for further measurements. The production yield of Gly-CNDs is approximately 30 %. A Lambda 35 UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Norwalk, USA), an FP-8500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Jasco Co., Oklahoma, USA), 4300 Handheld FTIR Spectrometer (Agilent Technologies Co., California, USA) and a transmittance electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2000FXII model, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) were used to characterize the isolated Gly-CNDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of caffeine content using the HPLC system
ce:section.ce:para: Caffeine content in foods was measured following the method described in ISO 20481 – 2008 (ISO 20481, 2017). For measurement, The ECS05 Gradient Analytical HPLC System (ECOM Co., Praha, Czech Republic) was operated with a UV–VIS PDA detector (ECDA2800, ECOM Co., Praha, Czech Republic) and Nucleosil C18 100 Å column 5 μm, 150 × 4.6 mm (Phenomenex Corporation, California, USA). Samples were measured at 25 °C using a mobile phase that contains acetonitrile (ACN) and 0.01 M phosphate buffer (pH 6.4) in a 90:10 (v/v) ratio with a flow rate of 2 mL/min and a run time of 8 min. The retention time of caffeine was observed to be around 5.3 – 5.6 min. Caffeine standard, purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company (Missouri, USA), was used to plot the standard curve.

The calibration curve was created using caffeine concentrations ranging from 0 to 2060 nM, prepared in triplicate. The method was validated by establishing the linear range of the analyte and calculating the limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ). The precision of the analysis was confirmed through recovery samples, which were prepared by adding varying concentrations of each analyte to real samples.

Three samples were collected to analyze the caffeine content using Gly-CNDs and HPLC. All samples were opened and sonicated for 1 hour to completely degases. The sample was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter (VWR International Co., Ltd., Pennsylvania, USA). Depending on the measurement method, samples were diluted using ultrapure water,

All experiments were performed in triplicate, and the data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical significance was determined using one-way ANOVA, and differences were considered significant at p < 0.05. The statistical analysis used SPSS software with 25.0 version (IBM Ltd., New York, USA). ImageJ software version 1.54D (New York, USA) was used to analyze and disperse particles from TEM pictures. GraphPad Prism version 9.0 (GraphPad Software Inc., California, USA) was used to construct all charts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The characterization of purified carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs) is comprehensively illustrated in Fig. 2. The TEM image with a 10 nm scale reveals that uniformly dispersed Gly-CNDs are roughly spherical, indicating successful synthesis (Fig. 2 A). The size distribution histogram shows a narrow size distribution centered around 4.2 ± 1.2 nm, highlighting the consistency in particle size (Fig. 2 B). The fluorescence properties of Gly-CNDs were scanned with the excitation wavelength ranging from 280 – 460 nm and the emission wavelength ranging from 288 – 748 nm. As a result (Fig. 2 C), the optimum excitation wavelength was recorded at 360 nm with the highest emission wavelength at 448 nm, similar to CNDs from the Maillard reaction (Nguyen et al., 2024c; Li et al., 2018). This tunable fluorescence is advantageous for optimizing detection sensitivity and selectivity. Fig. 2 D displays the UV–vis absorption spectra of the Gly-CNDs. It exhibits distinct peaks, with a prominent peak around 280 nm corresponding to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and a broad absorption tail into the visible range, suggesting surface defects and functional groups, similar to the previous studies about CNDs (Nguyen et al., 2024c; Li et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020). The Raman spectra of Gly-CNDs show two prominent peaks at around 1350 cm⁻¹ (D-band) and 1600 cm⁻¹ (G-band), confirming the graphitic structure of Gly-CNDs (Fig. 2 D), similarly with Raman spectra of nitrogen-doped CNDs (Zhao et al., 2025). However, the Raman spectra of Gly-CNDs are relatively weak and broad due to the presence of surface functional groups that mask the underlying carbon core. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured to understand the core structure of Gly-CNDs in Fig. 2 G. The XRD patterns of Gly-CNDs exhibit broad, diffuse peaks centered around 2θ = 25°, indicative of an amorphous carbon structure with a high degree of disorder. This is attributed to the presence of various functional groups and defects on the surface of CNDs. The elemental composition of Gly-CNDs presents in Fig. 2 E with the percentage of carbon (33.1 %) and nitrogen (18.2 %). To understand further the structure of Gly-CNDs, the FTIR spectra indicate significant changes compared to glycine, demonstrating structural transformation during pyrolysis (Fig. 2 F). The peak in the 1600–1650 cm⁻¹ and 1500–1400 cm⁻¹ range, corresponding to the COO⁻ asymmetric stretch in glycine (Groenewegen, 1972), appears more pronounced in CNDs, suggesting the formation of aromatic CC domain (1600–1650 cm⁻¹ region) (Ma et al., 2025), and indicating modifications in carboxyl functional groups (broadens in 1500–1400 cm⁻¹ range) (González-González et al., 2022). The peak at 1350 cm⁻¹ , attributed to CH/NH bending in glycine, is enhanced in CNDs, likely due to the formation of sp² carbon domains (Ferrari et al., 2003). An increase in the 1100–1050 cm⁻¹ range suggests the presence of C–N or C–O stretching linked to oxidized surface groups, confirming surface functionalization (Kolanowska et al., 2022). At 900 cm⁻¹ , glycine exhibits C–H or N–H wagging, which sharpens in CNDs due to surface modifications, likely from the introduction of amine or hydroxyl groups (Zhao et al., 2025; Groenewegen, 1972). Lastly, the 700–800 cm⁻¹ region, corresponding to C–H bending or skeletal vibrations, remains largely unchanged but shows minor shifts, indicating subtle molecular rearrangements (Zhao et al., 2025). These spectral changes reflect the transformation of glycine into CNDs through aromaticity development, sp² carbon formation, and oxygen/nitrogen surface functionalization.The HPLC-SEC-FD chromatogram of isolated Gly-CNDs is illustrated in Fig. 2 H. By calculating the molecular mesh of each peak, the Gly-CNDs were identified and collected as the first peak to obtain purified Gly-CNDs (Shafigulina et al., 2019). The sample was lyophilized and stored for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 investigates the stability of the glycine-derived carbon nanodots (Gly-CNDs) under various conditions relevant to practical applications. The Gly-CNDs exhibit good stability in different NaCl concentrations from 0.1 M to 1 M, with the fluorescence intensity remaining relatively constant (Fig. 3 A). This suggests that the Gly-CNDs are resistant to aggregation induced by salt, making them suitable for applications in physiological environments. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of Gly-CNDs remains largely unchanged over a 60-minute, indicating good stability over time (Fig. 3 B). However, their stability is pH-dependent, with optimal performance at acidic pH 4.0 (Fig. 3 C and Fig. 3 D). The fluorescence intensity of Gly-CNDs was observed to significantly decrease at alkaline conditions, suggesting a potential link to surface charge properties. Fig. 3 D presents the emission fluorescence spectra of the Gly-CNDs at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm but at various pH conditions (4.0–11.0). The emission peak remains around 440 nm across the different pH values, further highlighting the stability of the Gly-CNDs optical properties. Overall, Gly-CNDs exhibited excellent stability across various salt concentrations and over time, maintaining consistent performance under acidic pH conditions.

The examination of selectivity holds significant importance for each reaction system. In order to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method, the effects of common sweeteners (glucose, fructose), beverage additives (ascorbic acid, citric acid, benzoic acid (preservative, E210), some ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) and some toxins compounds (acrylamide, 5-HMF) on the reaction system were investigated. Fig. 4 illustrates that the interfering species did not significantly impact the reaction system due to the strong specificity of Gly-CDs for caffeine. Additionally, it demonstrated that the proposed method can selectively identify caffeine even in the presence of high concentrations (500 μM) of interfering species in beverages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Building the assay for measuring caffeine using carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 presents the emission spectra of carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs) at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, demonstrating the fluorescence response under different conditions: (1) Gly-CNDs in the presence of 5000 µM caffeine (red line), (2) only Gly-CNDs without caffeine (black line), and (3) a caffeine solution of 5000 µM without Gly-CNDs (green line). In Fig. 5, the emission spectrum of Gly-CNDs without caffeine (black line) exhibits a strong fluorescence peak with the maximum fluorescence intensity at 2232.82 (a.u.), indicating the typical fluorescence properties of the CNDs. When 5000 µM of caffeine is added to the Gly-CNDs solution (red line), a significant enhancement of fluorescence intensity is observed over than 50 % with the obtained maximum value at 3722.57 (a.u.), confirming the interaction between caffeine molecules and Gly-CNDs. Caffeine has a very low fluorescent intensity (green line) at the excitation wavelength at 360 nm. When the CNDs solution was added to the caffeine solution, the fluorescent intensity increased significantly to 50 % compared to the fluorescence of CNDs, and 4000 times compared to the fluorescence of caffeine. These results underscore the effectiveness of Gly-CNDs as a fluorescent probe for caffeine detection.

Fig. 6 illustrates the relationship between caffeine concentration and the fluorescence enhancement efficiency of carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs). Fig. 6 A presents a plot of quenching efficiency (F/F0) against caffeine concentration, showing a linear relationship over the range from 0 to 2060 nM. The linear fit equation
y = 0 . 008225 × X + 1 . 006
with a high correlation coefficient (R2=0.9929), indicating a strong linear dependency of fluorescence enhancement on caffeine concentration. The low of detection (LOD) is recorded with 36.5 nM caffeine concentration, and the limit of quantification (LOQ) is 122.1 nM. This high correlation suggests that Gly-CNDs are highly sensitive to caffeine, enabling accurate quantification over a wide concentration range. The linearity and sensitivity are crucial for developing a reliable sensing method for caffeine detection in various food matrices. The increase of emission spectra of Gly-CNDs in the presence of caffeine at concentrations ranging from 0 to 2060 nM illustrated in Fig. 6 B with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and in in Fig. 6 C for concentration ranging from 0 to 309 nM. The fluorescence intensity Gly-CNDs demonstrates a positive correlation with increasing concentrations of caffeine. This relationship is supported by consistent emission wavelengths recorded during measurements.

Table 1 compares different carbon nanodots (CNDs) used for detecting caffeine. It includes sulfur-doped CNDs (Nemati et al., 2018), iron-doped Red CNDs (Othman, 2024) and glycine-derived CNDs (this work). While sulfur-doped CNDs and iron-doped CNDs have a narrow linear range but high sensitivity with a LOD of 0.05 µM, in this study, glycine-derived CNDs have the broadest detection range and a LOD of 0.043 µM, making them versatile for various caffeine detection applications. Moreover, utilizing glycine and sugars as starting materials reduces the potential for toxicity, making it particularly suitable for use in the food industry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanisms for carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs) fluorescence enhancement by caffeine
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing mechanism of Gly-CNDs towards caffeine is characterized by a notable enhancement in fluorescence intensity, as evidenced by the linear increase in F/F0 with increasing caffeine concentration (Fig. 6 A). This suggests a direct interaction between Gly-CNDs and caffeine, leading to a quantifiable change in the nanodot's emissive properties. Characterization of the Gly-CNDs (Fig. 2) reveals a spherical morphology with an average size of 4.2 ± 1.2 nm (Fig. 2 A, B), indicating a high surface area conducive to interactions. The presence of abundant surface functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and nitrogen-containing moieties, is confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2 F). These groups likely play a crucial role in the interaction with caffeine. Given the observed fluorescence enhancement, we propose that caffeine molecules interact with these surface functional groups through hydrogen bonding, potentially restricting vibrational or rotational freedom and reducing non-radiative decay pathways. Furthermore, the presence of graphitic domains, inferred from the Raman spectra (Fig. 2 D), suggests the possibility of π-π stacking interactions with the aromatic rings of caffeine. This interaction, along with potential electrostatic interactions between the nitrogen-containing groups on Gly-CNDs and polar regions of caffeine, could contribute to surface passivation or structural rigidification, thereby enhancing radiative decay and increasing fluorescence. The observed linear fluorescence enhancement (Fig. 6 A) and the concentration-dependent emission spectra (Fig. 6 B, C) further support a direct and quantifiable interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for caffeine detection using carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal experimental conditions for utilizing carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs) to detect caffeine content were investigated, which resulted in Fig. 7 highlighting the effects of pH, incubation time, and temperature on the fluorescence intensity of Gly-CNDs in the presence of caffeine (1717 nM). First, the stability of Gly-CNDs in the presence of caffeine (1717 nM) in different pH conditions was assessed across a pH range of 3.0–11.0, where pH 3.0–5.0 is the most suitable pH range to measure caffeine (Fig. 7 A). The fluorescence significantly decreases at pH levels beyond 7, reaching minimal values at pH 9, 10, and 11. These findings are illustrated in Fig. 7 B, showing a significant reduction in emission spectra as the pH increases. This pattern indicates that Gly-CNDs exhibit good stability and strong fluorescence in mildly acidic to neutral conditions, typical for many food products. The reduced fluorescence at higher pH suggests a change in surface chemistry or a quenching effect (Głowacki et al., 2022). This is consistent with the reversible 'off-on' fluorescence switch observed in nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) between strongly acidic and alkaline solutions (Liu et al., 2016).

Subsequently, the impact of different incubation times on the fluorescence intensity of Gly-CNDs with the addition of 1717 nM caffeine at 40°C (Fig. 7 C) was investigated. The results indicated that the fluorescence intensity peaked at 25 minutes, signifying that this is the optimal incubation time for the interaction between Gly-CNDs and caffeine. After this peak, a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed, suggesting potential photobleaching or saturation effects of the Gly-CNDs over longer incubation periods. Additionally, it was observed that the optimal temperature for maximum fluorescence intensity ranged from 40 to 50°C (Fig. 7 D). Increasing the temperature beyond this range resulted in a steady decline in fluorescence intensity, indicating that higher temperatures may have an adverse effect on the fluorescence properties of Gly-CNDs. This could be attributed to thermal quenching effects or destabilization of the carbon nanodots at elevated temperatures (Yu et al., 2012). In conclusion, the best conditions for detecting caffeine using Gly-CNDs derived from glycines are a pH of 5.0, an incubation time of 25 minutes, and an incubation temperature of 40°C. These conditions produce the highest fluorescence intensity, making caffeine detection more sensitive and accurate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of caffeine in commercial beverages and compared with HPLC method
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 evaluates the application of Gly-CNDs for measuring caffeine in actual beverage samples, comparing the results with high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) as a reference method. Three energy drinks were selected for this measurement including sample 1: HELL energy drink- classic boxing kings editions (HELL Magyarország Ltd., Hungary); sample 2: XIXO energy drink original flavor (XIXO Company Ltd., Hungary); sample 3: energy drink apple flavor (Hansen Natural Company, USA) along with three type of tea inlcuding sample 4: green tea (TenRen Co., Taiwan); sample 5: black tea (TenRen Co., Taiwan); sample 6: olong tea (Cau Dat farm Co., Da Lat, Vietnam). In particular, one commercial fake coffee, which is coffee mixed with burnt corn, was collected from the Vietnam market (sample 7) to ensure that this method can be used to measure caffeine in coffee adulteration. Fig. 8 A displays the emission spectra of the energy drinks sample. The blue line represents the spectra of sample 1 without the presence of Gly-CNDs, while the red line represents the spectra of sample 1 with the addition of 75 ppm Gly-CNDs at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The spectrum with added Gly-CNDs (red line) exhibits a significant increase in fluorescence intensity, approximately eight times higher than the spectrum without Gly-CNDs (blue line). This marked enhancement confirms the sensitivity of Gly-CNDs to caffeine, as the Gly-CNDs interact with caffeine molecules, leading to fluorescence enhancement that can be detected and quantified. Furthermore, the caffeine concentration of three energy drink samples was measured using fluorescence-based and HPLC measurements (Fig. 8 B). There are no significant differences between the caffeine concentration of the three samples using the fluorescence-based measurements and HPLC results (p > 0.05). Table 2 validates the recovery of caffeine concentration in the samples. The recovery ranged from 95.902 ± 2.173 % to 102.703 ± 1.834 %, with the RSD% maintained from 1.272 % to 1.968 %. This demonstrates the effectiveness of Gly-CNDs as a reliable sensor for quantifying caffeine. This suggests that the Gly-CNDs can effectively be a sensing platform for detecting caffeine in real-world beverage samples.

This study successfully demonstrates the potential of carbon nanodots derived from glycine (Gly-CNDs) as an effective tool for rapidly detecting caffeine in beverage samples. The synthesized Gly-CNDs, with an average size of 4.2 ± 1.2 nm, exhibited remarkable stability across various conditions, including different NaCl concentrations, time durations, and pH levels. The fluorescence-enhancing property of these carbon nanodots in the presence of caffeine enabled a highly sensitive detection method, with a linear response observed within the caffeine concentration range of 39–2060 nM with a low LOD of 36.5 nM. Additionally, the Gly-CNDs maintained their stability and fluorescence emission characteristics across a pH spectrum of 3.0–5.0, ensuring consistent performance in diverse sample acidic environments. Comparative analysis with the HPLC method revealed no significant variance in caffeine concentration measurements, confirming the accuracy and reliability of the Gly-CNDs-based fluorescence technique (p > 0.05). To our knowledge, this study is the first article using carbon nanodots derived from food-grade material (glycine and sugar) to rapidly detect caffeine in foods with the broadest detection range and lowest LOD. This research highlights the feasibility of utilizing Gly-CNDs from coffee brews as a fast, cost-effective, and reliable approach for caffeine detection, presenting a promising alternative for the food and beverage industries.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

József Prokisch: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Hassan El-Ramady: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation. Nguyen H.H. Duyen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Stipendium Hungaricum scholarship, Hungary.
